summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 7 and 7 Plus, focusing extensively on the new black and jet black finishes, the upgraded camera systems, and the controversial new non-clicking home button. He traces Apple's evolving materials engineering from the original iPhone through the iPhone 7, arguing that the jet black finish is gorgeous but prone to micro-abrasions, while the matte black is the safer choice. The dual camera system on the 7 Plus is compelling but limited by the telephoto lens's smaller aperture and lack of OIS, and the flagship Portrait mode isn't shipping at launch. He theorizes the new solid-state home button exists primarily to address a global phenomenon where tens of millions of users avoid clicking the home button to preserve resale value, calling it the one thing about the iPhone 7 he doesn't like.
key_points: The jet black finish is the grippiest iPhone ever made but shows micro-abrasions within days, while the matte black is significantly darker than previous space gray and should age better

The iPhone 7 finally gets optical image stabilization for both stills and video, eliminating the Plus's previous exclusive camera advantage, while the 7 Plus adds a dual camera system with a 56mm telephoto lens

The new solid-state home button feels worse than the physical click it replaces, and Gruber theorizes it was designed to address tens of millions of users worldwide who avoid clicking the home button due to wear fears
Apple's iterative industrial design evolution is a sign of maturity, not stagnation — the changes in materials engineering alone justify the new generation
The Portrait mode feature that would most justify the 7 Plus isn't shipping at launch, making the size tradeoff harder to justify
money_quotes: Apple is getting better and better at making devices resistant to wear and tear.
I wouldn't be surprised if the news stories and pundit hot takes about jet black iPhone scratches and scuffs outnumber those about the Samsung Galaxy Note 7 literally exploding and hurting people.
Evolution isn't about jumping from one design to another, for the sake of novelty. It's about improvements.
Apple is now the leading camera company in the world, and the iPhone is fundamentally much more of a camera than it is a telephone.
The worst part is, I wouldn't be surprised if this no-click button doesn't change the behavior of these people. UI habits are hard to break, and superstitions are resistant to reason.
If you're going to have a bump, own the bump. The iPhone 7 owns its bump.
I consider myself a connoisseur of button clickiness.
It's not a hard decision because they're similar — it's a hard decision because both finishes are excellent, just excellent in different ways.
key_insight: The iPhone 7 represents Apple's mastery of materials engineering and iterative refinement, with the stunning new black finishes proving that meaningful design evolution doesn't require radical form factor changes — though the solid-state home button is a rare misstep born from solving a problem most Western users didn't have.
